coredata.dc:description: Comprehensive interpretation of photo-generated carrier dynamics in photocatalytic systems will strengthen its position as a candidate for future utilization and development of solar energy resources. As exploratory models, aggregated carbon dots (A-CDs) and dispersed carbon dots (D-CDs) were coated on the surface of g-C3N4 nanovesicles (CNNVS) to form A-CDs/CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS composite systems. Among of them, A-CDs/CNNVS exhibits a superior photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution of up to 24.8 mmol/g, which was about 2.03 and 1.23 times higher than that of CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS, respectively. Ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) demonstrates that the improved thermalization of photo-generated electrons and trapping behavior of cooled electrons via the introduction of A-CDs in A-CDs/CNNVS to complement the hot electrons of intrinsic excitation and electrons trapping of CNNVS at a higher extent in comparison of D-CDs/CNNVS. This work presents an in-depth insight to investigate CDs-based photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution system for realizing solar energy conversion.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Comprehensive interpretation of photo-generated carrier dynamics in photocatalytic systems will strengthen its position as a candidate for future utilization and development of solar energy resources. As exploratory models, aggregated carbon dots (A-CDs) and dispersed carbon dots (D-CDs) were coated on the surface of g-C3N4 nanovesicles (CNNVS) to form A-CDs/CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS composite systems. Among of them, A-CDs/CNNVS exhibits a superior photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution of up to 24.8 mmol/g, which was about 2.03 and 1.23 times higher than that of CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS, respectively. Ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) demonstrates that the improved thermalization of photo-generated electrons and trapping behavior of cooled electrons via the introduction of A-CDs in A-CDs/CNNVS to complement the hot electrons of intrinsic excitation and electrons trapping of CNNVS at a higher extent in comparison of D-CDs/CNNVS. This work presents an in-depth insight to investigate CDs-based photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution system for realizing solar energy conversion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven water-splitting into hydrogen has been extensively pursued to address the problem of energy shortage in an environmentally friendly way in the context of China's “dual-carbon” policy, which is highly dependent on the discovery of novel photocatalysts and the modification of existing photocatalysts [1]. Generally, typical photocatalytic reaction steps include photoexcitation of the semiconductor material, separation and migration of photo-generated charges, and surface catalytic reactions between carriers and reactant [2]. The surface reaction (ms-s) efficiency is dominated by the lifetime of the photoexcited charge (ps-ns) due to the fast time scale, while the initial state of the catalytic reaction can be directly determined by the effective electron concentration at the surface, which is also directly associated with the relaxation of excited state carriers [3]. In addition, the contact frequency and the contact velocity between the migrating carriers and the reactants also highly influence the rate of intermediate and H2 formation, thus affecting the kinetics of the surface catalytic reaction [4]. It demonstrates that the rate of the surface catalytic reaction of H2 production is closely related to the carrier kinetics and the kinetics of the surface catalytic reaction involved in the mass-transfer efficiency [5]. Consequently, optimizing the interfacial charge transfer process is recognized as a key means to effectively prolong the separated carrier life and extend the surface reaction rate [6].

Form the field of surface-science, the flow of high-energy hot electrons on the surface is not only a momentous factor affecting the surface properties of the material, but also can initiate and promote the chemical reaction process on the surface, which is an important driving force driving the surface chemical reaction [7]. Hence, it is essential to research and comprehend the hot carrier dynamics on the catalyst surface for revealing the mechanism of interaction of high-energy hot electrons with surface matter, the nature of surface chemical processes and develop novel surface science applications. In a heterogeneous photocatalytic system, semiconductor materials are excited by the high energy (luminous energy and thermal energy, etc.) to produce hot carriers, and then adjusts the photoelectric properties of the material in the process of hot carrier transmission and diffusion [8]. Moreover, in the absence of delicate design and precise synthesis for interfacial structure, the photocatalytic H2 production rate of the photocatalyst is directly dominated by the number of high-energy hot electrons, because the hot carrier relaxation is customarily much faster than the charge extraction during the reaction [9]. Unfortunately, transport and cooling of hot carriers in semiconductor photocatalytic reactions are actually competitive. The hot carriers may lose energy and cool to a relatively lower energy level through phonon scattering, interface scattering and radiative recombination, which are more likely to be captured by the large inter-band-gap trap states that introduced by the rough synthesis at the position of different energy difference (ΔE) from the edge of the conduction band (CB) during the charge transfer process [10]. This will lead to slow charge transfer or recombination of carriers at the band edge, which further affect the interface charge transfer characteristics and surface catalytic reactions because of the energy loss and reduced driving force of electrons [11]. Accordingly, maintaining sustained rapid transport of hot carriers is a core element in meeting the requirements of efficient photocatalytic reactions.

The photothermal synergistic catalytic system that couples photochemical and thermochemical conversion is an ideal strategy for extending the lifetime of hot electrons and achieving efficient energy conversion due to photothermal catalysis integrates the advantages of both photocatalysis and thermal catalysis to some extent [12,13]. Compared to traditional single photocatalytic systems, photothermal-assisted photocatalytic systems induce localized temperature elevation through interface modification, accelerating complex thermodynamic and kinetic transfer processes by increasing the entropy of chemical reactions (ΔS > 0), thereby enhancing the macroscopic reaction rate [14]. In particular, water splitting is a challenging uphill thermodynamic reaction with a high energy barrier, and providing a high-temperature, high-energy environment can increase the average kinetic energy of reactant molecules, accelerating the collision frequency and energy between reactant molecules, effectively promoting the completion of the photocatalytic H2 production reaction from water splitting [15]. Typically, the construction of a photothermal-assisted photocatalytic system is achieved by coupling photo-absorption components to efficiently capture light and complete the photothermal conversion to increase the reaction temperature of the system [16]. Additionally, the thermal energy generated in photothermal-assisted systems is often affected by temperature gradients and results in thermal radiation throughout the heterogeneous system, leading to energy loss [17]. According to the above point of views, an ideal photocatalyst for photothermal-assisted system should possess the following characteristics at least: (i) superior optical absorption for full carrier excitation and high-level energy injection; (ii) fast carrier separation and migration kinetics for facilitating the formation of the initial state; (iii) ingenious structural design for balancing the impact of heat loss and charge separation.

Carbon dots (CDs), emerging as a new class of carbon-based photothermal and photo/electroactive materials, have aroused worldwide research interests [18–20]. The synthesis of CDs from bottom-up causes by-products other than the target product, including molecular fluorophores and/or aggregated structures (molecular fluorophores attached to the core of CDs) in polymerization, crosslinking, and carbonation of organic molecules, which can be purified by dialysis to separate the CDs [21]. In this work, two heterogeneous photothermal-assisted photocatalytic systems were successfully constructed by coating two kinds of CDs with different states (aggregated CDs, A-CDs/dispersed CDs, D-CDs) on the surface of g-C3N4 nanovesicles (CNNVS) for investigating the effect of the photothermal behaviors of CDs in different states on the transfer of hot electrons and trapping state of cooled electrons of CNNVS. Ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) verified that heat-transfer channels and conductive channels were constructed by the high density of conjugated π-structures in the organic fluorophore molecules of A-CDs, realizing fast and efficient heat injection and electron transport in CNNVS to complement the intrinsic excitation of hot electrons and their lifetime extension in CNNVS. In addition, the vesicle structure fully receives and confines the heat injection from A-CDs thus effectively overcoming the thermal radiation and perfectly balancing the effects of heat loss and charge separation via analysis of COMSOL Multiphysics software. This work presents an in-depth insight to investigate CDs-based photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution system for realizing solar energy conversion, and could work as an amendment to published work that ignored the CDs purification procedure.
Melamine (C3H6N6, 99 %) Cyanuric acid (C3H3N3O3, 99 %), Urea (CH4N2O, 99 %) and Citric acid (C6H8O7 ≥ 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dispersed carbon dots (D-CDs) and aggregated carbon dots (A-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The A-CDs were prepared according to the previous report [22]. Briefly, 12 g of citric acid and 24 g of urea were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water and stirred to form a transparent solution. Then, the obtained solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm to remove large particle impurities and unreacted precursors, and further dried at 80 °C to form the A-CDs powder. In order to obtain D-CDs, the powder of A-CDs was diluted with water and dialyzed (MD34-12000) for 24 h to further purify to remove molecular fluorophore by-products, and the purified solution was dried at 80 °C to obtain the D-CDs powder.

Three-dimensional CNNVS were prepared by a facile thermal polymerization according to our previous work [23]. First, 1.26 g of melamine and 1.935 g of cyanuric acid were dissolved in 30 mL and 20 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), respectively, and stirred for 20 min to allow excellent dispersion. The solution was then slowly dropped into the melamine solution under stirring conditions, and the resulting milky solution was stirred for 2 h. After stirring, 100 mL of deionized water was added above solution and left for 8 h to form a white precipitate. Next, the precursor precipitate was centrifuged, dried and grounded. Finally, the resulting powder was placed in a muffle furnace and heated from room temperature to 550 °C at a heating rate of 2.3 °C/min and maintained for 4 h.

The synthesis of coating D-CDs or A-CDs on the surface of CNNVS to form D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS composites is similar to the synthetic method of CNNVS. First of all, 15 mg A-CDs or D-CDs powder was dispersed in 15 mL deionized water and stirred on magnetic stirrer to form A-CDs or D-CDs solution at a known concentration (1 mg/mL). The difference is that a certain proportion (1, 2, 3 and 5 mL) of A-CDs or D-CDs solution was added in a mixture of cyanuric acid solution and melamine solution and then stirred for 2 h. The subsequent steps are exactly the same as for the synthesis of CNNVS. The obtained samples were denoted as A-CDs/CNNVS-x or D-CDs/CNNVS-x, where x stands for the volume of A-CDs or D-CDs. When not specified, A-CDs or D-CDs refers to 1 mL. The specific test operation steps of photocatalytic H2 tests and other corresponding characterizations are provided in the Supporting Information .

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, A-CDs are synthesized by a conventional hydrothermal method using urea and citric acid as raw materials, which mainly include CDs and molecular fluorophore byproducts. These molecular fluorophores may be oxidized to become an aromatic amino acid chain and more readily co-assembled with CDs or via π-π stacking self-assembly to induced the aggregation behavior of CDs [24]. Among them, A-CDs were further dialyzed and most of the fluorophores were removed to obtain CDs in the dispersed state (D-CDs). In order to confirm the synthesis and existence state of the D-CDs/A-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed. As exhibited in Fig. 1b, the D-CDs displayed as monodisperse uniformly spherical particles, whose diameter is approximately 4 nm. In contrast, the A-CDs performed aggregation of some CDs to form CDs clusters (Fig. 1c) that maintained their same size and keeps indirect or contact with each other. Further observations of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with higher resolution for D-CDs and A-CDs present that a significant lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is consistent with the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [25–28]. Significantly, the discrepancy is the dense lattice arrangement of A-CDs, which is associated to the aggregation of CDs clusters, while indirect interactions in A-CDs suggest co-assembly of CDs with molecular fluorophores [29]. To determine the molecular fluorophores in the A-CDs, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out. As exhibited in Fig. 1d, the FT-IR spectra of D-CDs and A-CDs displayed similar characteristic absorption peaks, including the peaks at 3135, 1585 and 1400 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H, C = C bonds and N-containing aromatic rings and C-H bonds, respectively [30]. Note that compared to that of D-CDs, a new characteristic peak at 3440 cm−1 was found in FT-IR spectrum of A-CDs, belonging to the N-H bonds in molecular fluorophores [31]. In addition, the optical properties of D-CDs and A-CDs were analyzed by UV–Vis absorption spectra as exhibited in Fig. 1e, displaying that both the samples possess similar absorption band in the range of 200–400 nm. Specifically, two distinct absorption band at the peak of 232 nm is originated from the π–π* transitions of C = C in aromatic sp2 domains, while the peak at 334 nm is ascribed to the n–π* transitions of different surface state transitions associated with C = O on the surface of the CDs materials [32,33]. Furthermore, absorption peak at 600 nm in A-CDs could provide a strong piece of evidence of molecular fluorophores, meanwhile, the introduction of chromophores and co-chromophores from molecular fluorophores can effectively improve the visible light response of D-CDs. From the digital photos of aqueous A-CDs and D-CDs under UV (365 nm) light, a distinct blue fluorescence compared to the D-CDs solution, which can be attributed to the fluorescence effect of molecular fluorophores [34].

The schematic diagrams for synthesis routes of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS was given in Fig. 2 a. The D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS photocatalysts were produced via a template-free self-assembly synthesis process, including two steps: supramolecular self-assembly and thermal polymerization. In the early stage, the strong hydrogen bonds could be formed by the aerobic hydroxyl group of cyanuric acid, the amino group of melamine in the solution of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) [35,36]. Melamine and cyanuric acid are capable of forming sufficient self-assembly when saturated in DMSO solvent, while the addition of deionized water causes the mixture to form strong interactions that drive the arrangement of these organic molecules into supramolecular aggregates. At the same time, the van der Waals forces generated during stirring and the small amount of hydrogen bonding due to the surface functional groups between the A-CDs/D-CDs and the supramolecular aggregates contribute to the anchoring of the A-CDs/D-CDs [37,38]. During thermal polymerization, D-CDs/A-CDs and supramolecular aggregate form tighter connection, and structural collapse and gas impacts (such as NH3 and CO2) accompanied by the loss of material during pyrolysis results in large pores in the precursor, generating g-C3N4 with 3D porous nanovesicle structure. The microstructure of the prepared photocatalysts were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). It is clearly observed that the CNNVS is a 3D nanovesicle-like structure with fine pores on the surface (Fig. 2b). After modification of D-CDs/A-CDs, the D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS composites still maintain the nanovesicle structure (Fig. 2c and d), but the pores on the surface of the D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS are smaller, which may be due to the padding effect of D-CDs and A-CDs during high temperature thermal polymerization [39]. The porous structure of as prepared samples was subsequently further investigated by the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and displayed in Fig.S1. All photocatalysts were type IV isotherms in the presence of an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of unevenly sized holes in the CNNVS [40,41]. The increased surface area of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS in comparison of pristine CNNVS could prove that the intrinsic properties of CDs in provide additional surface area and active site of semiconductor photocatalysts. Besides, A-CDs presents the highest specific surface area (70.1 m2 g−1), indicating the great role of the fluorophore molecules, which possess a large molecular surface area, and when the fluorophore molecules interact with the CDs, they may form a covering or adsorption layer on the surface of the CNNVS as well as attaching to the surface as a functional unit to increase the specific surface area. TEM images (Fig. 2e-g) allow for a better visualization that the vesicle structure of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS are hollow, the wall thickness and surface pores size gradually decreased, which are consistent with the results of pore size distribution plots (Fig.S2). HRTEM images in Fig. 2h-g exhibits that compared to the CNNVS, CDs in the disperse state or the aggregated state was distributed on the surface of D-CDs/CNNVS or A-CDs/CNNVS composite and formed a lattice of 0.21 nm consistent with the (001) crystal surface of carbon [42], which initially confirms the introduction of the D-CDs/A-CDs into composite system. The high-angle annular dark field scanning TEM (HAADF-TEM) images in Fig.S3 and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) measurements in Fig.S4 reveal that the as-prepared samples contain C, N and O elements, and evenly distributed.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS photocatalysts at different temperatures was evaluated by using 25 mg of photocatalyst in an aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as sacrificial agent and 1 wt% Pt as co-catalyst, and compared with the capability of g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNNS) derived from melamine precursor. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the hydrogen production of CNNS reached 3.35 mmol/g within 2 h of irradiation at room temperature (t = 25 ℃). In contrast, the photocatalytic H2 production performance of CNNVS (12.1 mmol/g) with vesicle structure was significantly improved, which is attributed to the fact that the vesicular structure can effectively realize the multiple reflection and refraction of light in the cavity, generating more photons for participating in the proton reduction reaction to produce hydrogen [73]. With the addition of D-CDs and A-CDs, the hydrogen production rates of D-CDs/CNNVS (10.1 mmol/g/h) and A-CDs/CNNVS (12.4 mmol/g/h) at room temperature (t = 25 ℃) were further improved (Fig. 4b), due to the large number of electron-withdrawing functional groups and the conjugated π-electronic structure of the D-CDs and A-CDs [74]. In addition, photocatalytic hydrogen production yields and evolution rates of the prepared samples were also tested at the temperature of 5 °C with using a cooling recirculation system (as given in inset of Fig. 4a and b), the results show that both photocatalytic activity and hydrogen production decreased and maintained the same trend as room temperature, indicating that photocatalytic hydrogen evolution is highly dependent on the reaction temperature of this system. According to the previous report of our group, CDs, as graphene-like materials, possess great potential in the field of photothermal conversion [75], thus the change of water temperature in the photocatalytic composite system with or without loading two kinds of carbon dots were recorded and explored. As exhibited in Fig.S6, the temperature of the deionized water was only increased to 31.3 ℃ under 2 h of illumination, while the temperature of the CNNS solution was increased to 34 °C under the same conditions (Fig.S7). The temperature of the CNNVS-containing solution (38 °C, Fig. 3d) was significantly higher than the CNNS due to the porous structure of the vesicle that promotes multiple reflections of light to improve light utilization, while the cavity structure can be used as a nanoconfinement reactor with thermal insulation [76,77]. With the addition of D-CDs and A-CDs, the temperature of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS were significantly enhanced to 40.5 and 41.6 ℃ (Fig. 4e and f) respectively owing to absorption and emission properties of conjugated π-structures of D-CDs and A-CDs. Accordingly, the improved photothermal performance of D-CDs/CNNVS or A-CDs/CNNVS is not only ascribed to the special vesicle structure, but also due to the fact that D-CDs/A-CDs themselves can promote effective light absorption as well as photothermal conversion. In contrast, A-CDs possesses superior light capture capability and thermal conductivity owing to the presence of an abundance of conjugated π-structures as well as related groups in the molecular fluorophores [78]. In Fig. 4c, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of optimal A-CDs/CNNVS was up to 2.11 % recorded at the wavelength of 420 nm, which is better than other CN-based photocatalysts reported in recent years (Fig. 4g and Table S5). The results of the cyclic experiment in Fig. 4h suggests that there is no significant decline in the hydrogen production during the 5-cycle tests over the as-prepared A-CDs/CNNVS composite photocatalyst. Meanwhile, the XRD pattern (Fig.S8a), FT-IR spectrum (Fig.S8b) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig.S8c), SEM image (Fig.S9) and TEM image (Fig.S10) after photocatalytic reaction are almost the same, indicating that the microstructure of sample after photocatalysis was not destroyed, again confirming the excellent cycling stability of A-CDs/CNNVS composite photocatalyst.

To evaluate the effect of aggregated or dispersed state of CDs on the photothermal conversion ability, D-CDs and A-CDs powders were irradiated under full-spectrum irradiation and the corresponding temperature changes were recorded using a thermal camera (Fig.S11). It can be seen that the temperature of D-CDs powders increased dramatically from room temperature (27 ℃) to 121 ℃ within 180 s and reached steady. Compared with D-CDs, the temperature of A-CDs (142 ℃) rises fast and higher in the same condition, which shares a superior photothermal behavior of A-CDs in photothermal conversion [79]. Notably, upon four cycles of solar irradiation on and off, the steady temperatures of A-CDs for each cycle were almost unchanged (Fig.S12), demonstrating the excellent photothermal stability of the CDs/fluorophores hybrid material. After the recombination of CNNVS, the photothermal properties of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS were also significantly enhanced (as provided in Fig. 5 a). Under the same light intensity irradiation within 6 min, the temperature of the sheet-like CNNS is the lowest (58.4 °C), compared with the vesicle-like CNNVS, the temperature of which is significantly higher to 79.8 °C, which is due to the fact that the vesicle structure promotes multiple reflections of light in the cavity for effective utilization of light [80]. The sample surface temperatures of CNNVS are significantly increased upon coupling of both D-CDs and A-CDs, with the difference that the sharp increase in the temperature of D-CDs/CNNVS (92.2 °C) is due to the abundance of π-electronic structural units in the dispersed state of the D-CDs as well as the extension of the light absorption range by surface functional groups [81]. Besides, the higher temperature of A-CDs/CNNVS (93.8 °C) than that of D-CDs/CNNVS is attribute to the π-π stacking-induced aggregation of CDs in the conjugated system, which shortens the molecular spacing and improves the intermolecular interaction force, thus effectively promoting the photo-absorption, photothermal conversion and thermal stability [82]. In Fig. 5b, the temperature–time change curves for the different samples and calculated the average heating rate for each sample were further compared. The heating rate of A-CDs/CNNVS (13.16 ℃/min) is higher than that of D-CDs/CNNVS (12.86 ℃/min) and much higher than that of CNNVS (10.4 ℃/min) and CNNS (6.16 ℃/min), which is attributed to the superiority of the A-CDs with fluorophores. Meanwhile, the heating rate of the CNNVS is significantly faster than the CNNS, effectively indicating that the cavity structure of the vesicle facilitates efficient light utilization. During the tests, we also found that the cooling time of the vesicle-structured sample could be effectively prolonged by taking infrared images of A-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNS and calculating the cooling rate of A-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNS (Fig. 5c and d), which could reflect the superior insulating properties of the vesicles as nanoreactors [83]. The heat-collecting effect in the confined cavity is the key factor in improving the photocatalytic performance of the A-CDs/CNNVS photothermal nanoreactor. Although it is difficult to promote the temperature outside of the vesicles filled with a large amount of water, only a small amount of water can be heated inside of the vesicles, so the temperature of the internal space over A-CDs/CNNVS nanoreactor should be much higher than that of the external space. Unfortunately, the individual A-CDs/CNNVS is only a few hundred nanometers in size, making it difficult to directly measure the temperature of their internal space. Consequently, a two-dimensional (2D) finite element model is utilized to explore the temperature shift of the individual A-CDs/CNNVS nanoreactor from inside to outside under irradiation, and the natural convective heat transfer in hollow nanospheres in solution can be described using the non-isothermal flow multi-physics field coupling module in COMSOL Multiphysics software [84]. Under the initial condition (0 s), the water temperature inside and around A-CDs/CNNVS is the same as that of 298 K (≈ 25 °C) (Fig.S13), and the temperature inside and on the surface of A-CDs/CNNVS can reach up to 350 K (≈ 78 °C) after 1800 s of illumination (Fig. 5e), and the temperature of the water in the external ambient decreases drastically with the increase of the distance from A-CDs/CNNVS. Although the single-particle model ignores the complex interactions between multiple vesicles, the results still strongly prove that the internal temperature of A-CDs/CNNVS under light irradiation is much higher than that of the external ambient water. Significantly, owing to the enhanced photothermal effect in the confined space, the water molecules around the high-temperature A-CDs/CNNVS reactor move faster, which is conducive to increasing the chances of photo-generated carriers, thus enhancing the photocatalytic reaction rate by promoting the local mass transfer kinetics [85]. The photocurrent density curves, fitted electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) plots and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of A-CDs/CNNVS at different temperatures are given in Fig. 5f-h, respectively. As the temperature increases, the photocurrent is significantly enhanced and the transfer impedance becomes significantly smaller, which effectively confirming that the increase in temperature leads to the acceleration of carrier separation and photo-induced charge transfer [86,87]. Meanwhile, the temperature also affects the overpotential of the photocatalyst, which means that the photothermal effect can effectively improve the hydrogen production kinetics and promote the proton reduction reaction [88–91]. Simultaneous comparison of the photocurrent response, EIS curves and LSV plots of all the samples at room temperature (Fig.S14, Fig.S15 and Fig.S16) reveals that with the introduction of D-CDs/A-CDs, the photocurrent response and transfer impedance of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS relative to the pristine CNNVSs are significantly improved due to the conjugated π-structure of the D-CDs/A-CDs, which effectively increase the electrical conductivity of the material [92]. Meanwhile, the overpotential required for CNNVS can reach −2.16 V at 10 mA cm−2, whereas that for D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS can be significantly reduced to −1.99 and −1.94 V, respectively, suggesting that aggregation of CDs can more effectively overcoming kinetic barriers and improve the reaction kinetics of photocatalytic H2 generation.

Transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) is considered to be a powerful tool for monitoring the relaxation dynamics of excited-state carriers in any single system or heterogeneous system from ultrafast time scale [93]. Herein, the transients in electromagnetic spectral range of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS from UV to Vis under the pump excitation of 343 nm were probed by TAS, spanning picosecond (∼ps) to nanosecond (∼ns) time windows. In general, negative signals are attributed to ground-state bleaching (GSB) and/or stimulated radiation (SE)[94]. Among them, a broad negative photoinduced transient bleaching signal can be observed at 400 nm and displays two peaks around 392 and 455 nm (Fig. 6 a-c). Electrons from the ground state (S0) upon irradiation by non-resonant high-energy pump photons (343 nm, 3.87 eV) are excited to higher single-linear state excited states. Bleached valleys at around 455 nm can be attributed to the band-edge singlet exciton (BSE)[95], which originates from the bleaching of the S1-S0 transition because the ground-state bleached signal at 455 nm (2.73 eV) happens to be in close proximity to the position of the optical band gap of the CNNVS (Fig. 3h). Based on the characterization of the band-edge bleach signal, another strong light-induced bleach signal centered at 392 nm was attributed to the formation of higher-order single-linear-state excitons (HSE), which is originated from the participation of higher-order excited states in the g-C3N4 conducting system [96]. The process of Coulomb binding interaction between photo-generated electrons and holes to form the HSE requires a certain amount of thermal absorption, while the HSE may be cooled to the band edge by a phonon-mediated internal conversion process, which is a heat release transversion [97]. Consequently, the changes of HSE and BSE signals can fully reflect the transport and cooling process of hot electrons. Relative to the clear HSE and BSE signals of CNNVS, the BSE signal of D-CDs/A-CDs almost disappears after the formation of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS, which suggests that the cooling behavior of the hot electrons is almost disappeared, which is a significant indication that the hot electrons are efficiently transported to the surfaces of the CDs. In order to probe deeply into the states of HSE and BSE, the carrier kinetic profiles of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS were extracted at 392 and 455 nm (Fig. 6d and e) and fitted them with the triple-exponential decay function, and the fitting parameters are provided in Table S6 and S7. The TA kinetics of the HSE signals of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS at 392 nm were compared, which indicates that the kinetics of D-CDs/CNNVS exhibit a sizeable fast decay compared to the original CNNVS, strongly suggesting that the hot electrons can be transferred quickly in the D-CDs/CNNVS composite photocatalytic system. In comparison with D-CDs/CNNVS, the HSE signal of A-CDs/CNNVS system has a faster decay kinetics, which may be due to the π-π conjugation in the A-CDs of the aggregated state, forming a continuous electron transport channels and improving the electron transport efficiency [98]. The TA kinetics trend of the BSE signal at 455 nm is the same as that at 392 nm, which is be due to the reduced number of cooling hot electrons. As exhibited in Fig. 6f, the relaxation mechanism of carriers generally follows the following processes: (i) the thermalization and cooling process of photo-generated electrons, that is, the photo-generated electrons are first thermalized into HSE through Coulomb interactions and further cooled to form BSE through a phonon-mediated internal conversion process; (ii) binding process of cooled band-edge excitons, that is, the electrons of CB edge are trapped at different depths to form shallowly trapped electrons and deeply trapped electrons. (iii) Above trapped electrons can recombine with holes by radiative/non-radiative means. Minimal τ1 of A-CDs/CNNVS confirms the ability of A-CDs to promote hot electron migration, and BSE signals can be also perfectly fitted by three decay time components, where τ1 and τ2 can be attributed to shallow and deep trap state trapping of electrons, respectively, and τ3 represents the recombination of photo-generated electrons [99]. With the introduction of D-CDs/A-CDs, the BSE signals τ1 and τ2 components of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS are drastically narrowed, confirming that the number of cooled electrons caught in the trapped state is low and most of the electrons are transferred to the surface of the CDs in the form of hot electrons. The results of the above analysis indicate that ultrafast hot electron transfer from high-energy states suppresses carrier relaxation from band-edge states [100]. Unlike the first negative signal, the second negative signal starts around 695 nm (Fig. 6g and i), which overlaps with the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectral range of the prepared samples in Fig.S17 (450 nm-800 nm), so we attribute it to SE, since the main feature of SE is the presence of fluorescence generation reflecting the recombination process of photoinduced carriers and/or bound excitons [101]. As exhibited in Fig.S17, the major PL emission peaks of CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS samples are at about 500 nm, and the intensity of the PL peaks of D-CDs/CNNVS was sharply attenuated compared with that of CNNVS, which confirms that the electron extraction of D-CDs facilitates the efficient separation of photo-induced carriers. Differently, the PL peaks of A-CDs/CNNVS showed a sharp decrease in intensity and a large redshift compared to CNNVS and D-CDs/CNNVS. The former due to the superior charge extraction and transport properties of A-CDs, and the latter due to the incorporation of the heptazine unit by the C = C organic component, resulting in the expansion of π-electronic conjugated system. Likewise, the SE signal of A-CDs/CNNVS (Fig. 6i and l, ∼700 nm) is highly suppressed than CNNVS (Fig. 6g and j, ∼735 nm) and D-CDs/CNNVS (Fig. 6h and k, ∼728 nm), providing another evidence that the conjugated π units of the aggregated A-CDs promotes efficient carriers’ separation in the composite system. The positive signal is generally considered to be due to the excited state absorption (ESA) of photo-generated trapped electrons in the mid-bandgap trap state [102]. As provided in the pseudo-colored TAS spectra of CNNVS, D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS (Fig. 6j-l), it is intuitively observed that the intensities of positive signals of D-CDs/CNNVS and A-CDs/CNNVS are highly diffuse and becomes significantly stronger than CNNVS, and the carrier relaxation times of A-CDs/CNNVS (2.4 ps-2254.1 ps) and D-CDs/CNNVS (0.2 ps-1324.8 ps) are significantly prolonged compared with that of the pristine CNNVS (1.9 ps-345.2 ps), suggesting that more photo-generated electrons are involved in the photocatalytic reaction [103]. Furthermore, the carrier kinetic curves at 785 nm were extracted and fitted to evaluate the trapping characteristics of the photo-induced carriers, the fitting parameters are given in Table S8. Two fitted components τ1 and τ2 were assigned to the shallow trapping time of the electron in a trap state at the edge of the CB and to the deep trapping time in a long-range trap state at the inter-band gap, respectively, and τ 3 denotes the photo-generated carriers’ complexation time [104]. The large τ1 (93.6 ps) and τ2 (266.9 ps) time constants of CNNVS indicate that a large portion of the electrons in CNNVS fall into the trapped state from the CB and that there are fewer electrons in the shallow trapped state, and most of the trapped electrons are deep trapped electrons, which are more prone to be consumed by rapid recombination with holes on the VB prior to the catalyzed reaction at the surface, which is unfavorable for the photocatalytic reaction. Nevertheless, after the introduction of D-CDs on the surface of CNNVS, the significant reduction of τ1 component (42.3 ps) and τ2 component (72.3 ps) for D-CDs/CNNVS not only indicates that the number of electrons are trapped in the inter-band-gap trap states due to the transport of photo-generated electrons to the surface of D-CDs, which facilitates surface separation of electron-hole pairs, but also effectively indicates that the vast majority of the trapped electrons sink into the shallow trap state, making it more susceptible to surface catalytic reactions. The significant lengthening of the τ3 component of D-CDs/CNNVS also strongly demonstrates the superior relaxation ability of photo-generated electrons. Notably, the self-assembly of CDs to form aggregates under π-π stacking interactions can increase the size and mass of the CDs and form additional π-conjugated structures in the A-CDs, which effectively improves the CNNVS optical properties and carrier transport capacity. Thus, with the introduction of A-CDs to form A-CDs/CNNVS, the photogenerated electron transport and transfer efficiency is significantly improved compared to that of D-CDs/CNNVS, which can be reflected in the further reduction of τ1 (35.4 ps) and τ2 (32.6 ps) for A-CDs/CNNVS photocatalyst. In addition, the increase of shallowly trapped electrons and further decrease of deeply trapped electrons in A-CDs/CNNVS could greatly improve the selectivity and efficiency of the proton reduction reaction and inhibit the recombination of carriers, which can be reflected in the τ3 component of A-CDs/CNNVS (5439.1 ps). Besides, to accurately quantify the electronic lifetime of the catalysts on a time scale, it was also possible to fit the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves in Fig.S18 according to triple-exponential function [105]. The fitting results show that the average lifetime (Tave) of D-CDs/CNNVS (1.07 ns) and A-CDs/CNNVS (1.14 ns) are significantly higher than that of CNNVS (1.02 ns), which implies that the electrons can be stored in the excited state, increasing the likelihood of charge separation, and that the low electron-hole complexation results in a higher concentration of lone pairs of electrons [106]. Finally, in contrast to the decay rate of the kinetic profile of CNNVS, it is observed that A-CDs/CNNVS decays much faster than that of D-CDs/CNNVS (Fig.S19), which on the one hand suggests that the transfer of the shallow trapped electrons with the incorporation of A-CDs/D-CDs, and on the other hand, effectively suggests that the aggregated state A-CDs are significantly better than dispersed state D-CDs in terms of electron transfer.

Summarizing the above discussion, a possible mechanism for aggregated state A-CDs to promote the improvement of hydrogen H2 performance of CNNVS was proposed (Fig. 7 ). The photo-physics of the A-CDs/CNNVS interface is a collective effect of three phenomena: (i) the thermalization and transfer process of photo-generated electrons. Unlike normal semiconductors with purely photoexcitation process, due to the vesicle structure and the presence of aggregated state A-CDs, the absorption of photon energy excitation by A-CDs/CNNVS is accompanied by a thermal excitation process, which excites the electrons from the VB to the CB. Photo-generated electrons are thermalized into HSEs via Coulomb interactions and are rapidly transferred to A-CDs surface or surface catalytic reactions under the conduction of high-density conjugated π-units [107]; (ii) the cooling and capturing process of photo-generated hot electrons. In addition to the transfer of some of the hot electrons, another portion of the electrons are further cooled to the band edge through a phonon-mediated internal conversion process after thermalization, culminating in the formation of low-energy BSEs [108]. Eventually, the cooled electrons become trapped in inter-band-gap trap states at the edge of the CB, and the deeper the capture, the greater the energy loss of the electrons and the lower the drive for the catalytic reaction. Generally, deep trapped electrons are mostly recombined with holes of VB by radiative/non-radiative means due to the excessive energy required [109]. The deep trap state for CNNVS is usually located below the hydrogen reduction level, giving it a low level of photocatalytic activity, which is detrimental to the formation of the target product. In this case, the lower positive reduction potential required for the shallow capture of electrons is further transferred to the surface of the A-CDs after hot electron transfer for additional proton reduction reactions. (iii) transition of the electron trapped states. Due to the precision of the synthesis process, impurities/defects may be present in the semiconductor material thereby creating impurity energy levels between the bands, leaving the probability and depth of electron trap at a relatively high level. The addition of A-CDs improves the electron trap state achieving a transition from deep to shallow electron capture state. Moreover, CDs possess unique energy level structures and surface chemistry, and their surface energy levels can interact weakly with the bandgap energy levels of semiconductors, which may induce the formation of shallow trapping states [110]. Other notable factors affecting the extent of the trapped state are bandgap energy and temperature. The bandgap of semiconductor material customarily cannot be changed by CDs themselves, but aggregated state A-CDs significantly increase the surface temperature of the system and enhance the thermal excitation capability of semiconductor materials. For one thing, by giving the electrons more energy to jump directly to higher energy level or to leave the capture state, for another, the energy gap (band gap energy) of the semiconductor material shrinks with increasing temperature, which means that at higher temperatures, electrons require less energy to jump to the CB, further increasing the likelihood that they may leave the trapped state. (iv) creating a redistribution of the electron cloud. CDs act as transport channels for electrons in semiconductors, and this electron migration affects the distribution of the electron cloud [111], and the concentration of the electron cloud away from inter-band traps and the high rate of electron migration reduces the probability of deep electron trapping, which is one of the reasons why the degree of deep trapping of aggregated state carbon dots is greatly reduced with respect to dispersed state D-CDs, as the large number of conjugated π-structures of the fluorophore molecules promotes the reconfiguration of the electron cloud and the enhancement of the electron transport kinetics [112].

In summary, the carrier dynamics process in a heterogeneous model of coupled photothermal aggregated A-CDs or dispersed D-CDs with CNNVS photocatalyst was observed by tracking the separated charge signals using ultrafast TAS spectral analysis. Detailed experimental studies demonstrate that the excellent photocatalytic activity of optimal A-CDs/CNNVS composite system in this work mainly stems from three aspects: (i) the optical absorption range of CNNVS exhibits pronounced red-shifted due to inducing effective electron delocalization by abundant conjugated π-structures contained in organic fluorophore molecules of A-CDs, and was further extended by the formation of π-π stacking in organic conjugated molecular aggregates, resulting in promoting non-radiative decay to generate heat and realizing efficient photothermal conversion upon aggregation; (ii) thermalization of photo-generated electrons is optimized via fast and efficient heat injection through heat transfer channels built with conjugated π-structures of A-CDs and the molecular fluorophores, and further rapidly transferred to A-CDs surface or surface catalytic reactions under the conduction of high-density conjugated π-units to complement the hot electrons of intrinsic excitation; (iii) As the conjugated π structure of A-CDs promotes the reconstruction of the electron cloud and the energy enhancement, the transition causes the cooling electron capture state, thus optimizing the cooling electron capture behavior of CNNVS. In addition, CNNVS as vesicular confinement nanoreactors not only achieve multiple reflections of incident light, but also possess the excellent photo-thermal insulation in the composite system. This study provides a reference for the better design of a CDs-based photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 evolution system in the future.
